g.data-narration: Welcome to Thread Priorities in Java - learn how to control thread execution order
Thread priority determines relative scheduling importance. Higher priority threads get more CPU time from the JVM scheduler
g.text: Thread priority is an integer value (1-10) that indicates to the JVM scheduler
how important a thread is. Higher priority threads receive more CPU time.

g.data-narration: Thread priorities help control execution order in multithreaded programs for better performance and responsiveness
g.text: • Control execution order of concurrent threads
• Ensure critical tasks get CPU time first

g.data-narration: Java provides three constants: MIN_PRIORITY=1 for lowest, NORM_PRIORITY=5 as default, MAX_PRIORITY=10 for highest importance
Use setPriority() method to assign priority values between 1 and 10 to threads
g.text: Use the setPriority() method to assign priority:

g.data-narration: Use getPriority() method to retrieve the current priority value of any thread
Visual representation: low priority threads get narrow CPU time slices, high priority threads get wider slices from the scheduler
Thread priority behavior varies across operating systems. The JVM uses priorities as hints, not guarantees for thread execution order
g.text: Thread priority is a hint to the OS scheduler, not a guarantee:
• Linux: Priority may have minimal effect (CFS scheduler)

g.data-narration: This example demonstrates priority effects by comparing counters between low and high priority threads running simultaneously
g.text: Create two threads with different priorities and compare their execution:

g.data-narration: The PriorityDemo thread class uses a volatile running flag and increments a counter while the loop continues
The run method contains the thread's task - incrementing a counter in a loop controlled by the running flag
Essential thread control: start() begins execution, sleep() pauses temporarily, join() waits for thread completion
Daemon threads are low-priority background threads that don't prevent JVM from exiting when main program completes
g.text: Background threads that don't prevent JVM from exiting
Use case: monitoring, logging, garbage collection

g.data-narration: In the example, the high priority thread typically counts more than the low priority thread due to receiving more CPU time
Quick reference: priority values range from 1 to 10 with MIN=1, NORM=5 as default, MAX=10
Key takeaways: remember priority range 1-10, MIN=1 NORM=5 MAX=10, volatile for shared variables, priorities are platform-dependent hints
g.text: ✓ Use setPriority() to set, getPriority() to retrieve
✓ volatile keyword ensures visibility across threads
✓ Daemon threads don't prevent JVM exit
What is the thread priority range?

g.data-narration: In summary, thread priorities provide a way to hint to the scheduler which threads are more important for optimal application performance
g.text: Thread priorities help control execution order in multithreaded applications.
Use them wisely - they're hints to the OS scheduler, not guarantees!
Practice coding examples to solidify your understanding
